text: VO2max is not only an indicator of endurance performance but also a strong predictor of cardiovascular disease and mortality.
Lifelong endurance exercise can attenuate the rate of age-related VO2max decline, with VO2max values remaining higher than the general population across all ages.
A linear decrease in VO2max with aging is possible if physical exercise loads are kept consistently high throughout the entire lifespan.
A centenarian cyclist has been recorded with a VO2max of 35 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, demonstrating that exceptionally high aerobic capacity is maintainable into extreme old age with consistent training.

Lifelong exercise can attenuate or prevent most age-related physiological declines affecting VO2max, including reductions in cardiovascular function, blood O2 carrying capacity, skeletal muscle capillary density, and oxidative capacity.
Maximum heart rate decline with aging appears to be one physiological change that lifelong exercise cannot prevent, even in highly trained masters athletes.
